mn15:1.2: At one time the Venerable Mahāmoggallāna was dwelling among the Bhaggas at Susumāragiri, in Bhesakaḷā’s Deer Park.
mn15:1.3: There the Venerable Mahāmoggallāna addressed the *bhikkhus*:
mn15:1.5: “Friend,” those *bhikkhus* replied to the Venerable Mahāmoggallāna.
mn15:2.2: ‘Let the venerables speak to me; I am one to be spoken to by the venerables,’ yet he is hard to admonish, possessing qualities that make one hard to admonish, impatient, not receiving instruction respectfully, then his companions in the holy life do not think he should be spoken to, do not think he should be instructed, and do not think trust should be placed in that person.
mn15:3.1: And what, friends, are the qualities that make one hard to admonish?
mn15:3.10: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.11: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is angry, and because of anger harbors resentment.
mn15:3.12: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is angry, and because of anger harbors resentment—
mn15:3.13: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.14: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is angry, and because of anger becomes entangled.
mn15:3.15: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is angry, and because of anger becomes entangled—
mn15:3.16: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.17: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is angry, and under the sway of anger lets speech burst out.
mn15:3.18: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is angry, and under the sway of anger lets speech burst out—
mn15:3.19: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.2: Here, friends, a *bhikkhu* has bad desires, having fallen under the sway of bad desires.
mn15:3.20: Again, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* rebuts the corrector.
mn15:3.21: In that, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* rebuts the corrector—
mn15:3.22: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.23: Again, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* disparages the corrector.
mn15:3.24: In that, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* disparages the corrector—
mn15:3.25: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.26: Again, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* turns it back on the corrector.
mn15:3.27: In that, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* turns it back on the corrector—
mn15:3.28: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.29: Again, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* shuffles from one point to another, diverts the discussion elsewhere, and displays anger, hostility, and resentment.
mn15:3.3: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* has bad desires, having fallen under the sway of bad desires—
mn15:3.30: In that, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* shuffles from one point to another, diverts the discussion elsewhere, and displays anger, hostility, and resentment—
mn15:3.31: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.32: Again, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* does not give an explanation regarding the matter.
mn15:3.33: In that, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* does not give an explanation regarding the matter—
mn15:3.34: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.35: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is denigrating and insolent.
mn15:3.36: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is denigrating and insolent—
mn15:3.37: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.38: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is envious and stingy.
mn15:3.39: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is envious and stingy—
mn15:3.4: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.40: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.41: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is deceitful and crafty.
mn15:3.42: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is deceitful and crafty—
mn15:3.43: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.44: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is rigid and arrogant.
mn15:3.45: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is rigid and arrogant—
mn15:3.46: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.47: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is attached to his own views, taking hold of them tightly, hard to let them go.
mn15:3.48: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is attached to his own views, taking hold of them tightly, hard to let them go—
mn15:3.49: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.5: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is self-exalting, disparaging others.
mn15:3.50: These, friends, are called the qualities that make one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.6: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is self-exalting, disparaging others—
mn15:3.7: this too is a quality that makes one hard to admonish.
mn15:3.8: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is angry, overcome by anger.
mn15:3.9: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is angry, overcome by anger—
mn15:4.1: But even if, friends, a *bhikkhu* does not invite criticism:
mn15:4.2: ‘Let the venerables speak to me; I am one to be spoken to by the venerables,’ yet he is easy to admonish, possessing qualities that make one easy to admonish, patient, receiving instruction respectfully, then his companions in the holy life think he should be spoken to, think he should be instructed, and think trust should be placed in that person.
mn15:5.1: And what, friends, are the qualities that make one easy to admonish?
mn15:5.10: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.11: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not angry, not harboring resentment because of anger.
mn15:5.12: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not angry, not harboring resentment because of anger—
mn15:5.13: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.14: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not angry, not becoming entangled because of anger.
mn15:5.15: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not angry, not becoming entangled because of anger—
mn15:5.16: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.17: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not angry, not letting speech burst out under the sway of anger.
mn15:5.18: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not angry, not letting speech burst out under the sway of anger—
mn15:5.19: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.2: Here, friends, a *bhikkhu* does not have bad desires, and has not fallen under the sway of bad desires.
mn15:5.20: Again, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* does not rebut the corrector.
mn15:5.21: In that, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* does not rebut the corrector—
mn15:5.22: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.23: Again, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* does not disparage the corrector.
mn15:5.24: In that, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* does not disparage the corrector—
mn15:5.25: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.26: Again, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* does not turn it back on the corrector.
mn15:5.27: In that, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* does not turn it back on the corrector—
mn15:5.28: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.29: Again, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* does not shuffle from one point to another, does not divert the discussion elsewhere, and does not display anger, hostility, and resentment.
mn15:5.3: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* does not have bad desires and has not fallen under the sway of bad desires—
mn15:5.30: In that, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* does not shuffle from one point to another, does not divert the discussion elsewhere, and does not display anger, hostility, and resentment—
mn15:5.31: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.32: Again, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* does give an explanation regarding the matter.
mn15:5.33: In that, friends, when corrected by a corrector, a *bhikkhu* does give an explanation regarding the matter—
mn15:5.34: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.35: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not denigrating, not insolent.
mn15:5.36: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not denigrating, not insolent—
mn15:5.37: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.38: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not envious, not stingy.
mn15:5.39: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not envious, not stingy—
mn15:5.4: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.40: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.41: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not deceitful, not crafty.
mn15:5.42: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not deceitful, not crafty—
mn15:5.43: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.44: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not rigid, not arrogant.
mn15:5.45: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not rigid, not arrogant—
mn15:5.46: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.47: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not attached to his own views, not taking hold of them tightly, easy to let them go.
mn15:5.48: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not attached to his own views, not taking hold of them tightly, easy to let them go—
mn15:5.49: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.5: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not self-exalting, not disparaging others.
mn15:5.50: These, friends, are called the qualities that make one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.6: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not self-exalting, not disparaging others—
mn15:5.7: this too is a quality that makes one easy to admonish.
mn15:5.8: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not angry, not overcome by anger.
mn15:5.9: In that, friends, a *bhikkhu* is not angry, not overcome by anger—
mn15:6.1: There, friends, a *bhikkhu* should infer about himself by himself in this way:
mn15:6.10: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘I will not be angry, not overcome by anger.’
mn15:6.11: ‘This person who is angry, and because of anger harbors resentment — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me;
mn15:6.12: and if I too were angry, and because of anger harbored resentment, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:6.13: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘I will not be angry, not harboring resentment because of anger.’
mn15:6.14: ‘This person who is angry, and because of anger becomes entangled — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me;
mn15:6.15: and if I too were angry, and because of anger became entangled, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:6.16: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘I will not be angry, not becoming entangled because of anger.’
mn15:6.17: ‘This person who is angry, and under the sway of anger lets speech burst out — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me;
mn15:6.18: and if I too were angry, and under the sway of anger let speech burst out, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:6.19: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘I will not be angry, and I will not let speech burst out under the sway of anger.’
mn15:6.2: ‘This person who has bad desires, who has fallen under the sway of bad desires — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me;
mn15:6.20: ‘This person who, when corrected by a corrector, rebuts the corrector — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me;
mn15:6.21: and if, when corrected by a corrector, I too were to rebut the corrector, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:6.22: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘When corrected by a corrector, I will not rebut the corrector.’
mn15:6.23: ‘This person who, when corrected by a corrector, disparages the corrector — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me;
mn15:6.24: and if, when corrected by a corrector, I too were to disparage the corrector, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:6.25: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘When corrected by a corrector, I will not disparage the corrector.’
mn15:6.26: ‘This person who, when corrected by a corrector, turns it back on the corrector — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me;
mn15:6.27: and if, when corrected by a corrector, I too were to turn it back on the corrector, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:6.28: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘When corrected by a corrector, I will not turn it back on the corrector.’
mn15:6.29: ‘This person who, when corrected by a corrector, shuffles from one point to another, diverts the discussion elsewhere, and displays anger, hostility, and resentment — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me;
mn15:6.3: and if I too had bad desires and had fallen under the sway of bad desires, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:6.30: and if, when corrected by a corrector, I too were to shuffle from one point to another, divert the discussion elsewhere, and display anger, hostility, and resentment, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:6.31: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘When corrected by a corrector, I will not shuffle from one point to another, I will not divert the discussion elsewhere, and I will not display anger, hostility, and resentment.’
mn15:6.32: ‘This person who, when corrected by a corrector, does not give an explanation regarding the matter — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me;
mn15:6.33: and if, when corrected by a corrector, I too were not to give an explanation regarding the matter, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:6.34: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘When corrected by a corrector, I will give an explanation regarding the matter.’
mn15:6.35: ‘This person who is denigrating and insolent — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me;
mn15:6.36: and if I too were denigrating and insolent, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:6.37: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘I will not be denigrating, not insolent.’
mn15:6.38: ‘This person who is envious and stingy — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me;
mn15:6.39: and if I too were envious and stingy, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:6.4: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘I will not have bad desires; I will not fall under the sway of bad desires.’
mn15:6.40: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘I will not be envious, not stingy.’
mn15:6.41: ‘This person who is deceitful and crafty — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me;
mn15:6.42: and if I too were deceitful and crafty, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:6.43: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘I will not be deceitful, not crafty.’
mn15:6.44: ‘This person who is rigid and arrogant — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me;
mn15:6.45: and if I too were rigid and arrogant, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:6.46: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘I will not be rigid, not arrogant.’
mn15:6.47: ‘This person who is attached to his own views, taking hold of them tightly, hard to let them go — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me;
mn15:6.48: and if I too were attached to my own views, taking hold of them tightly, hard to let them go, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:6.49: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘I will not be attached to my own views, not taking hold of them tightly, easy to let them go.’
mn15:6.5: ‘This person who is self-exalting, disparaging others — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me;
mn15:6.6: and if I too were self-exalting and disparaging others, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:6.7: Knowing this, friends, a *bhikkhu* should arouse this intention in his mind [*citta*]: ‘I will not be self-exalting, not disparaging others.’
mn15:6.8: ‘This person who is angry, overcome by anger — this person is displeasing and disagreeable to me.
mn15:6.9: And if I too were angry, overcome by anger, then I too would be displeasing and disagreeable to others.’
mn15:7.1: There, friends, a *bhikkhu* should examine himself by himself in this way:
mn15:7.10: ‘I am self-exalting, disparaging others,’ then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should strive for the abandoning of those very bad, unwholesome *dhammas*.
mn15:7.11: But if, friends, while examining himself, a *bhikkhu* knows this:
mn15:7.12: ‘I am not self-exalting, not disparaging others,’ then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should dwell with that very *pīti* and gladness, training by day and by night in wholesome *dhammas*.
mn15:7.13: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* should examine himself by himself in this way:
mn15:7.15: If, friends, while examining himself, a *bhikkhu* knows this:
mn15:7.16: ‘I am angry, overcome by anger,’ then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should strive for the abandoning of those very bad, unwholesome *dhammas*.
mn15:7.17: But if, friends, while examining himself, a *bhikkhu* knows this:
mn15:7.18: ‘I am not angry, not overcome by anger,’ then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should dwell with that very *pīti* and gladness, training by day and by night in wholesome *dhammas*.
mn15:7.19: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* should examine himself by himself in this way:
mn15:7.2: ‘Am I one with bad desires, fallen under the sway of bad desires?’
mn15:7.20: ‘Am I angry, harboring resentment because of anger?’
mn15:7.21: If, friends, while examining himself, a *bhikkhu* knows this, ‘I am angry, harboring resentment because of anger,’ then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should strive for the abandoning of those very bad, unwholesome *dhammas*.
mn15:7.22: But if, friends, while examining himself, a *bhikkhu* knows this, ‘I am not angry, not harboring resentment because of anger,’ then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should dwell with that very *pīti* and gladness, training by day and by night in wholesome *dhammas*.

mn15:7.28: ‘I am not angry, not becoming entangled because of anger,’ then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should dwell with that very *pīti* and gladness, training by day and by night in wholesome *dhammas*.

mn15:7.56: ‘When corrected by a corrector, I do not shuffle from one point to another, I do not divert the discussion elsewhere, and I do not display anger, hostility, and resentment,’ then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should dwell with that very *pīti* and gladness, training by day and by night in wholesome *dhammas*.

mn15:7.78: ‘I am deceitful and crafty,’ then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should strive for the abandoning of those very bad, unwholesome *dhammas*.
mn15:7.79: But if, friends, while examining himself, a *bhikkhu* knows this:
mn15:7.80: ‘I am not deceitful, not crafty,’ then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should dwell with that very *pīti* and gladness, training by day and by night in wholesome *dhammas*.
mn15:7.81: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* should examine himself by himself in this way:
mn15:7.83: If, friends, while examining himself, a *bhikkhu* knows this:
mn15:7.84: ‘I am rigid and arrogant,’ then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should strive for the abandoning of those very bad, unwholesome *dhammas*.
mn15:7.85: But if, friends, while examining himself, a *bhikkhu* knows this:
mn15:7.86: ‘I am not rigid, not arrogant,’ then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should dwell with that very *pīti* and gladness, training by day and by night in wholesome *dhammas*.
mn15:7.87: Again, friends, a *bhikkhu* should examine himself by himself in this way:
mn15:7.88: ‘Am I attached to my own views, taking hold of them tightly, hard to let them go?’
mn15:7.89: If, friends, while examining himself, a *bhikkhu* knows this:
mn15:7.9: If, friends, while examining himself, a *bhikkhu* knows this:
mn15:7.90: ‘I am attached to my own views, taking hold of them tightly, hard to let them go,’ then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should strive for the abandoning of those very bad, unwholesome *dhammas*.
mn15:7.91: But if, friends, while examining himself, a *bhikkhu* knows this:
mn15:7.92: ‘I am not attached to my own views, not taking hold of them tightly, easy to let them go,’ then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should dwell with that very *pīti* and gladness, training by day and by night in wholesome *dhammas*.
mn15:8.1: If, friends, while examining himself, a *bhikkhu* sees all these bad, unwholesome *dhammas* in himself as not abandoned, then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should strive for the abandoning of all these bad, unwholesome *dhammas*.
mn15:8.2: But if, friends, while examining himself, a *bhikkhu* sees all these bad, unwholesome *dhammas* in himself as abandoned, then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should dwell with that very *pīti* and gladness, training by day and by night in wholesome *dhammas*.
mn15:8.3: Suppose, friends, a woman or a man, young, youthful, fond of adornment, were examining the reflection of their own face in a mirror that was pure and bright, or in a clear bowl of water. If they were to see there dirt or a blemish, they would strive for the removal of that very dirt or blemish.
mn15:8.4: But if they were not to see there dirt or a blemish, they would be pleased for that very reason:
mn15:8.5: ‘It is a gain for me indeed! It is pure indeed for me!’
mn15:8.6: In the same way, friends, if while examining himself a *bhikkhu* sees all these bad, unwholesome *dhammas* in himself as not abandoned, then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should strive for the abandoning of all these bad, unwholesome *dhammas*.
mn15:8.7: But if, friends, while examining himself a *bhikkhu* sees all these bad, unwholesome *dhammas* in himself as abandoned, then, friends, that *bhikkhu* should dwell with that very *pīti* and gladness, training by day and by night in wholesome *dhammas*.”
mn15:8.8: This is what the Venerable Mahāmoggallāna said.
mn15:8.9: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* delighted in the Venerable Mahāmoggallāna’s words.
